channel.title: Ilia Klishin Fact-Based Digest
channel.description: Fact-based multilingual digest with original source links.

item.title: Putin-Macron talks and the Russia expertise gap
item.description:
In this Feb. 14, 2022 Moscow Times opinion piece, Ilya Klishin argues that a small factual error in foreign reporting around Putin-Macron talks exposed a larger expertise problem in commentary about Russia. The trigger is a repeated claim that Putin quoted a specific punk song after his meeting with Emmanuel Macron.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2022/02/14/putin-macron-talks-highlight-gap-in-russia-expertise-a76375

item.title: Mass protests: lessons from a decade
item.description:
This Dec. 10, 2021 Moscow Times article revisits the 2011-2012 fair-elections protests a decade later and asks whether the movement truly "failed" or instead reshaped the political field in slower ways. Klishin traces the sequence from mass mobilization to repression: criminal cases, police violence, pressure on media owners, and harassment of public figures.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2021/12/10/russias-mass-protests-10-years-on-finding-hope-in-apparent-defeat-a75781

item.title: Commentary on pressure against independent media institutions
item.description:
how media freedom is framed through evidence, chronology, and attributable sourcing. The narrative avoids reductive labels and shows how wording choices can widen or narrow trust gaps in high-pressure media cycles. This card summarizes "Commentary on pressure against independent media institutions" published by The Moscow Times on 2021-08-24.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2021/08/24/the-kremlin-is-coming-for-media-one-by-one-and-society-is-helpless-to-stop-it-a74879

item.title: The Magic Mirror of Russian Public Opinion
item.description:
how public-opinion narratives are shaped by institutions, incentives, and media distribution logic. The narrative avoids reductive labels and shows how wording choices can widen or narrow trust gaps in high-pressure media cycles. This card summarizes "The Magic Mirror of Russian Public Opinion" published by The Moscow Times on 2021-07-12.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2021/07/12/the-magic-mirror-of-public-opinion-a74492

item.title: The Dilemma of the Great Terror: How to Report Roman Protasevich's Confession?
item.description:
In this 2021-06-07 The Moscow Times media ethics article, the central argument is how media ethics are tested when reporting on high-stakes coerced-confession footage. The text rebuilds the discussion through dated events, named actors, and publication context so readers can separate reported facts from interpretation. Instead of categorical labeling, it emphasizes proportional language and verification, especially when audiences face uncertainty and rapid narrative shifts.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2021/06/07/the-dilemma-of-the-great-terror-how-to-report-roman-protasevichs-confession-a74125

item.title: Let's Wait Until After the Elections
item.description:
how election-timing rhetoric can postpone decisions and normalize strategic waiting. The narrative avoids reductive labels and shows how wording choices can widen or narrow trust gaps in high-pressure media cycles. This card summarizes "Let's Wait Until After the Elections" published by The Moscow Times on 2021-05-17.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2021/05/17/lets-wait-until-after-the-elections-a73915

item.title: Luiza Rozova and the Helpless Intellectual
item.description:
In this 2021-03-19 The Moscow Times elite discourse article, the central argument is how elite discourse reveals limits of symbolic criticism when institutional accountability is weak. The text rebuilds the discussion through dated events, named actors, and publication context so readers can separate reported facts from interpretation. Instead of categorical labeling, it emphasizes proportional language and verification, especially when audiences face uncertainty and rapid narrative shifts.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2021/03/19/luiza-rozova-and-the-helpless-intellectual-a73301

item.title: Four Ways the Kremlin Could Block Western Social Networks in Russia
item.description:
which technical and political mechanisms can be used to restrict social-network access. The narrative avoids reductive labels and shows how wording choices can widen or narrow trust gaps in high-pressure media cycles. This card summarizes "Four Ways the Kremlin Could Block Western Social Networks in Russia" published by The Moscow Times on 2021-02-12.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2021/02/12/four-ways-the-kremlin-could-block-western-social-networks-in-russia-a72920

item.title: The Capitol Storming Gives Russians an Escape From Their Reality
item.description:
In this 2021-01-14 The Moscow Times comparative media framing article, the central argument is how coverage of the Capitol attack was reframed for Russian audiences as comparative narrative relief. The text rebuilds the discussion through dated events, named actors, and publication context so readers can separate reported facts from interpretation. Instead of categorical labeling, it emphasizes proportional language and verification, especially when audiences face uncertainty and rapid narrative shifts.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2021/01/14/the-capitol-storming-gives-russians-an-escape-from-their-reality-a72598

item.title: Warning! An Army of Russian Trolls Has Invaded TikTok
item.description:
how platform dynamics and troll methods move from domestic messaging into wider information spaces. The narrative avoids reductive labels and shows how wording choices can widen or narrow trust gaps in high-pressure media cycles. This card summarizes "Warning!
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2020/12/18/warning-an-army-of-russian-trolls-has-invaded-tiktok-a72410

item.title: The Moscow Times (2020-11-20) - Why Russians Dislike Good Russians In American Films
item.description:
In this 2020-11-20 The Moscow Times cultural representation article, the central argument is how cultural symbols and representation frames affect trust, identity, and audience reaction. The text rebuilds the discussion through dated events, named actors, and publication context so readers can separate reported facts from interpretation. Instead of categorical labeling, it emphasizes proportional language and verification, especially when audiences face uncertainty and rapid narrative shifts.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2020/11/20/why-russians-dislike-good-russians-in-american-films-no-less-than-bad-russians-a72097

item.description:
how cultural symbols and representation frames affect trust, identity, and audience reaction. The narrative avoids reductive labels and shows how wording choices can widen or narrow trust gaps in high-pressure media cycles. item.title: Anonymous Telegram Channels Are Shaping Russia's Daily Life
item.description:
In this 2020-09-28 The Moscow Times platform influence article, the central argument is how platform dynamics and troll methods move from domestic messaging into wider information spaces. The text rebuilds the discussion through dated events, named actors, and publication context so readers can separate reported facts from interpretation. Instead of categorical labeling, it emphasizes proportional language and verification, especially when audiences face uncertainty and rapid narrative shifts.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2020/09/28/anonymous-telegram-channels-are-shaping-russias-reality-a71588

item.title: Belarus Coverage Framing: The Moscow Times (2020-08-14) - Why Do Russians Resent Western Media Over The
item.description:
why protest outcomes that look like defeat can still produce long-term civic learning. The narrative avoids reductive labels and shows how wording choices can widen or narrow trust gaps in high-pressure media cycles. This card summarizes "Belarus Coverage Framing: The Moscow Times (2020-08-14) - Why Do Russians Resent Western Media Over The" published by The Moscow Times on 2020-08-14.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2020/08/14/why-do-russians-resent-western-media-over-the-protests-in-belarus-a71155

item.title: LA Times coverage referencing TV Rain soldier investigations
item.description:
In this 2015-09-14 Los Angeles Times external validation article, the central argument is how evidence on Russian soldiers in Ukraine was verified, documented, and later cited by external institutions. The text rebuilds the discussion through dated events, named actors, and publication context so readers can separate reported facts from interpretation. Instead of categorical labeling, it emphasizes proportional language and verification, especially when audiences face uncertainty and rapid narrative shifts.
Original source: https://www.latimes.com/world/europe/la-fg-russia-soldier-20150914-story.html

item.title: Freedom House referenced TV Rain reporting on Russian soldiers in eastern Ukraine
item.description:
Freedom House’s Nations in Transit 2015 report on Russia provides institutional documentation of political repression, media pressure, and war-related information control during 2014. For this digest context, the key section is Independent Media and related civil-society analysis. The report states that authorities intensified pressure on independent outlets, including action against Dozhd TV, legal constraints on broadcasters and bloggers, and broader restrictions on critical reporting.
Original source: https://freedomhouse.org/country/russia/nations-transit/2015

item.title: Guardian New East: 30 under 30 profile entry
item.description:
In this 2015-06-08 The Guardian professional profile article, the central argument is how profile coverage connected media work with technical contributions to protest-era coordination. The text rebuilds the discussion through dated events, named actors, and publication context so readers can separate reported facts from interpretation. Instead of categorical labeling, it emphasizes proportional language and verification, especially when audiences face uncertainty and rapid narrative shifts.
Original source: https://www.theguardian.com/world/2015/jun/08/30-under-30-moscows-young-power-list

item.title: Guardian profile notes role in protest-era digital infrastructure
item.description:
why protest outcomes that look like defeat can still produce long-term civic learning. The narrative avoids reductive labels and shows how wording choices can widen or narrow trust gaps in high-pressure media cycles. This card summarizes "Guardian profile notes role in protest-era digital infrastructure" published by The Guardian on 2015-06-08.
Original source: https://www.theguardian.com/world/2015/jun/08/30-under-30-moscows-young-power-list

item.title: English press archive on intimidation claims tied to activism
item.description:
how intimidation and harassment patterns around critics were documented in international rights reporting. The narrative avoids reductive labels and shows how wording choices can widen or narrow trust gaps in high-pressure media cycles. This card summarizes "English press archive on intimidation claims tied to activism" published by News24 on 2015-04-29.
Original source: https://www.news24.com/archives/city-press/anti-putin-activist-complains-of-security-harassment-20150429

item.title: A verifiable public-speaking record linked to event pages, videos, and program
item.description:
In this 2014-11-28 Diez / TEDx Chisinau coverage public speaking article, the central argument is a verifiable public-speaking record linked to event pages, videos, and program context. The text rebuilds the discussion through dated events, named actors, and publication context so readers can separate reported facts from interpretation. Instead of categorical labeling, it emphasizes proportional language and verification, especially when audiences face uncertainty and rapid narrative shifts.
Original source: https://diez.md/2014/11/28/tedxchisinau-live-propaganda-in-social-media-with-ilya-klishin-tedxchisinau/

item.title: TV Rain launched "Our Soldiers" civilian investigation
item.description:
This August 2014 TV Rain project page introduces "Our Soldiers," a public-facing investigation into reports of Russian servicemen killed, missing, or captured near the Ukrainian border during a period of official denials. The framing is explicit: the goal is not rhetorical escalation but factual clarification of identities, numbers, and circumstances. The page states that if authorities withhold information, independent verification must be built from open submissions, witness reports, and case-level documentation.
Original source: https://tvrain.tv/blog/blog_mihaila_zygarja/nashi_soldaty_vyjasnjaem_sudbu_rossijskih_voennyh_okazavshihsja_na_granitse_s_ukrainoj-435/

item.title: Early warning analysis on Russian troll expansion
item.description:
In this 2014-05-21 The Moscow Times disinformation analysis article, the central argument is how platform dynamics and troll methods move from domestic messaging into wider information spaces. The text rebuilds the discussion through dated events, named actors, and publication context so readers can separate reported facts from interpretation. Instead of categorical labeling, it emphasizes proportional language and verification, especially when audiences face uncertainty and rapid narrative shifts.
Original source: https://www.themoscowtimes.com/2014/05/21/the-kremlins-trolls-go-west-a35651

item.title: TEDx speaker profile: Ilya Klishin
item.description:
TEDxChisinau documentation works as a verifiable event record and speaker-profile source rather than a long-form analytical text. It provides structured metadata that supports biography and timeline consistency: location (Chisinau), event date (September 23, 2012), venue details, and program framing under the theme "Next Level. " The event description follows a motivation-and-action register, while the speaker section contains a concise profile tied to media roles and protest-era digital organization in 2011-2012.
Original source: https://www.ted.com/tedx/events/3947

item.title: HRW report on harassment of critics includes Klishin case context
item.description:
In this 2012-03-01 Human Rights Watch human rights documentation article, the central argument is how intimidation and harassment patterns around critics were documented in international rights reporting. The text rebuilds the discussion through dated events, named actors, and publication context so readers can separate reported facts from interpretation. Instead of categorical labeling, it emphasizes proportional language and verification, especially when audiences face uncertainty and rapid narrative shifts.
Original source: https://www.hrw.org/news/2012/03/01/russia-harassment-critics

item.title: International reporting on pressure linked to protest organizing
item.description:
why protest outcomes that look like defeat can still produce long-term civic learning. The narrative avoids reductive labels and shows how wording choices can widen or narrow trust gaps in high-pressure media cycles. This card summarizes "International reporting on pressure linked to protest organizing" published by The Guardian on 2012-02-02.
Original source: https://www.theguardian.com/world/2012/feb/02/russian-police-accused-scare-tactics
